q_K8iFQAAAAJ.keywords: Cognitive Systems, Artificial Intelligence in Education, Human-AI Teaming, Concept Formation, Intelligent Tutoring Systems, Apprentice Learner Models, Student Modeling, Incremental Learning, Tutor Authoring, Probabilistic Concept Formation
q_K8iFQAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Christopher J. MacLellan is an **Assistant Professor** at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group focused on building computational models and interactive systems at the intersection of *cognitive science*, *artificial intelligence*, and *education*. His work centers on developing and evaluating theories and systems that enable <u>intelligent tutoring</u>, <u>incremental concept formation</u>, and effective <u>human–AI teaming</u>. He combines mechanistic cognitive models (e.g., simulated learners and concept formation algorithms) with applied systems (authoring tools and tutor platforms) to_close the loop_ between learning theory and educational technology.

---

## Research Areas
MacLellan’s research spans several tightly connected areas: the design and evaluation of **intelligent tutoring systems** and authoring tools (e.g., SimStudent and Apprentice-style tutor builders), the development of *cognitive models* and simulated learners that both explain human learning and drive adaptivity in tutors, methods for <u>incremental concept formation</u> in structured and visual domains (e.g., TRESTLE and Cobweb-based extensions), and the study of **human–machine teaming**—how agents can be designed to augment team competencies and collaborate effectively with people. His recent work also explores how modern generative models can be integrated into tutor authoring and feedback generation (e.g., research on LLM-based feedback and generative approaches to educator-driven tutor creation), and on evaluation frameworks and testbeds for treating AI agents both as tutors and as students (e.g., Tutorgym and Val). Across these topics he emphasizes connecting computational models to empirical educational data and practical authoring workflows, as exemplified in papers such as "The Apprentice Learner Architecture: Closing the loop between learning theory and educational data" (2016), "Domain-general tutor authoring with apprentice learner models" (2022), and "Improving teamwork competencies in human-machine teams: Perspectives from team science" (2021).

---

## Notable Works
- "Improving teamwork competencies in human-machine teams: Perspectives from team science" (2021) — A perspective piece that synthesizes team science and AI methods to identify design principles and computational techniques for enabling effective *human–machine teaming*, shaping subsequent work on asymmetric cooperative games and team-centric evaluation.
- "The Apprentice Learner Architecture: Closing the loop between learning theory and educational data" (2016) — Introduces an architecture that operationalizes learning theories in simulated learners to both test cognitive hypotheses and automate elements of tutor authoring, bridging theory-driven models and applied ITS development.
- "Domain-general tutor authoring with apprentice learner models" (2022) — Demonstrates how apprentice-style simulated learners can be used to support *domain-general* tutor authoring, reducing the expertise barrier for creating adaptive instructional systems and informing later efforts in generative and educator-driven tutor interfaces.

---

## Academic Background
MacLellan’s body of work reflects interdisciplinary training across **cognitive systems, artificial intelligence,** and **learning sciences**; he is currently an Assistant Professor at Georgia Tech, where he directs research on computational models of learning and applied educational technologies. Over the past decade he has authored influential contributions on simulated learners, concept formation (e.g., TRESTLE, Cobweb4V), tutor authoring (SimStudent, Apprentice-based toolchains), and human–AI teaming, accumulating over 980 citations for his work to date. He maintains an active lab and code/paper repository (see his homepage: https://chrismaclellan.com/) and collaborates broadly with researchers in education, HCI, and AI. His publications span theory development, algorithmic innovations, and practical systems for authoring and evaluating tutors, positioning him at the nexus of theory-driven cognitive modeling and deployable educational AI.

r9K3MQwAAAAJ.keywords: Cryptography, Security, Searchable encryption, Symmetric encryption, Secure messaging, Ratcheted key exchange, Memory-bounded cryptography, Algorithm-substitution attacks, Honey encryption, Quantum key-length extension
r9K3MQwAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Joseph Jaeger is an **Assistant Professor** in the School of Computer Science at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group in applied and theoretical **cryptography** and **security** (homepage: http://www.cc.gatech.edu/~josephjaeger). His work spans both *practical systems* and *provable-security theory*, with particular emphasis on <u>efficient encrypted search at scale</u>, <u>secure messaging and ratcheting</u>, and <u>security under resource-bounded/adaptive adversaries</u>. Jaeger’s publications have attracted broad attention (total citations: **1,836**) for combining rigorous definitions and reductions with implementations and system analyses.

---

## Research Areas
Jaeger’s research sits at the intersection of systems-oriented cryptography and formal security. He has advanced the theory and practice of <u>searchable encryption</u>—designing dynamic, scalable constructions and practical data structures for servers holding tens of billions of encrypted record–keyword pairs ("Dynamic searchable encryption in very-large databases: Data structures and implementation"). He has formalized and proven security goals for modern secure messaging by studying <u>ratcheted encryption</u> and channel safety under state compromise ("Ratcheted encryption and key exchange: The security of messaging", "Optimal channel security against fine-grained state compromise: The safety of messaging"). Other threads include the study of <u>algorithm-substitution attacks</u> and covert subversion ("Mass-surveillance without the state: Strongly undetectable algorithm-substitution attacks"), the development of *memory-tight* reductions and proofs that account for attacker memory limits ("The memory-tightness of authenticated encryption", "Hiding in plain sight: memory-tight proofs via randomness programming"), and work on concrete/expected-time security and post-quantum considerations ("Expected-time cryptography: generic techniques...", "Quantum key-length extension"). Throughout, Jaeger frequently blends new definitional frameworks (e.g., adaptive compromise models, multi-user/multi-challenge notions) with concrete constructions and implementation-minded evaluations.

---

## Notable Works
- "Dynamic searchable encryption in very-large databases: Data structures and implementation" (2014) — a foundational practical/theoretical treatment of *dynamic symmetric searchable encryption* that scales to tens of billions of record–keyword pairs and provides asymptotically optimal server-side costs, bridging cryptographic definitions and deployable data structures.  
- "Pact: Privacy sensitive protocols and mechanisms for mobile contact tracing" (2020) — an influential, policy- and systems-oriented set of protocols and mechanisms aimed at enabling large-scale, privacy-preserving contact tracing during the COVID-19 pandemic, demonstrating Jaeger’s ability to apply cryptographic thinking to urgent real-world problems.  
- "Ratcheted encryption and key exchange: The security of messaging" (2017) — a rigorous formalization and provable-security treatment of ratcheting techniques used in modern secure messaging, providing syntax, definitions, and constructions that clarify and strengthen the foundations of end-to-end message security.

## Academic Background
Jaeger is an Assistant Professor at Georgia Tech’s School of Computer Science, where he directs a research program combining cryptographic theory and system-level security. His publication record shows sustained presence in premier cryptography venues (CRYPTO, EUROCRYPT, TCC and related conferences) and collaboration across systems and theory communities. Jaeger’s work has been widely cited and has influenced both academic directions (memory-tight proofs, adaptive compromise models, authenticated encryption analyses) and practical systems (searchable encryption deployments, messaging protocol analyses, privacy-sensitive contact-tracing proposals). He holds advanced training in computer science with a focus on cryptography and has a track record of mentoring students and contributing to community resources (e.g., protocol design and publicly available implementations) that bridge theory and practice.
